That was the crime he actually committed according to the arrest.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And what was -- what crime did he commit and do?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, the court said he didn't commit any --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Was it vehicular traffic?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: The court said he committed none. He was -- that's why.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: No, no I am talking about the trial courts and the evidence --

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Oh! The Trial Court.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, the Trial Court I presume thought that he was still a member of the group that was blocking the traffic and was for that reason entitled to be convicted I suppose. I have no way of knowing, I mean he didn't say.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Neither do we have anyway of knowing, do we?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: You mean, as to Phifer.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: No sir, but the Court of Appeals has dispensed with that question --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, but my point is this. If Phifer is not guilty under this charge and this conviction, how can we convict Shuttlesworth?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, the only thing I can say is this, the Court of Appeals construed the evidence to mean that he was never arrested for that. He was never arrested for that. He was arrested according to that view point for coming back not for the original set of circumstances out of which Mr. Shuttlesworth was arrested, but for the fact he came back.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But the trial court found that he was --

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: He found guilty.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He was convicted of the very same offenses that Mr. Shuttlesworth [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: The trial court found him guilty.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And he was convicted at the very same offenses that the trial court.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: The trial court found guilty, correct yes, that's true. The trial court found him guilty. But as I say, the Court of Appeals didn't agree at all with that, because they said the Byars was clearly, was contrary to that view. There was another word to the Court of Appeals found the facts entirely or differently in the two cases. And in the Phifer case, they said that he did not do anything at the time he was arrested, except refused to move away from Shuttlesworth which was not -- which was not related to traffic and nobody contented it was, not even officer Byars. In other words, the trial court may have made a mistake, but if so, that mistake was corrected by the Circuit Court, by the Court of Appeals, and is no longer as I see it in this Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Let me see if I understand those facts. Shuttlesworth was arrested and they called the police.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: And the officer took him to curb.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: And then while he and the officer were standing there waiting for patrol.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Mr. Phifer who had left the place where the obstruction was, he had gone somewhere else.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: He came back.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: He came up and commenced the talking --

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: -– with Mr. Shuttlesworth and the officer said that you can't talk with Mr. Shuttlesworth, Mr. Shuttlesworth is under arrest.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: And he continued to talk when the officer warned him again --

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: -– to move on and quit talking to him, and when he warned him three times. It was only then, that he arrested Phifer.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir, that's right. It's clear, that he -- that if the officer had been arresting him for the original blocking and obstructing, he should have arrested him when the man first walked up and said you're under arrest for participating in this blocking and obstructing, but he didn't do it. He said move away, go ahead, get away from here.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Then he had been arrested for the blocking; he arrested him when he arrested Shuttlesworth.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, I think he said that Phifer had disappeared and gotten away and he didn't know how to find him while he was engaged in arresting Reverend Shuttlesworth, he did not know where Phifer was. But the point is though certainly when he first walked back, when he came back to the scene, if he were being arrested for the offense that he originally was said to have been involved in, he should have been arrested immediately when he walked up that you are under arrest for participating in this blocking, but that was not the case. He was not under arrest.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What do the complaint say? Didn't the complaint charged him with blocking traffic?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: The complaint, yes, the complaint --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And what was he tried for, he was tried for that, yes he was tried for that.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes he was tried –-

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He was convicted of that wasn't he?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: You mean the trial Court?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, that's true.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So, how can you say that he wasn't arrested for that reason?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, I think it's just a question of his erroneously being arrested and his erroneously being convicted and the Court of Appeals cleared it up and said so, that he was wrongly arrested.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Is it like Thompson was convicted?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir. They certainly just didn't have anything there, because you didn't arrest him of what you charged him with, so you have to remove, and that's what they did.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The Court of Appeals as understand the opinion [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: I beg your pardon, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The Court of Appeals as I understand from the opinion which you had before me at this situation, that he had been arrested according to them and they quoted here, because they told him not to talk to Shuttlesworth.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Who was then a prisoner.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He insisted that he could talk to Shuttlesworth. They didn't let him do it, he just has to be arrested.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Whereupon they arrested him.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Then the Court of Appeals said that he was not blocking any traffic at the time he came up to talk to Shuttlesworth.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Surely, yes sir, right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And this conviction cannot stand.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: So that they held that under the fact, he had not violated either one, they said either one of the ordinances.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Right, yes sir, that's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's the difference between the two --

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: That is the difference.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: According to what the Court held.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir, yes sir, that is exactly. They said Reverend Shuttlesworth violated them and Phifer did not.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: This is a [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir he was there and at what point he moved, I don't know.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: He had been there, yes, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, at the very time of the arrest, I would not say, because the officer said that at that time either the other members of the group had moved or were in the process of moving except Reverend Shuttlesworth, the third time.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: So that actually Mr. Shuttlesworth is arrested for having allegedly [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, I think he had. He had, yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: How long have these ordinances been on the books down there in Birmingham?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, I looked into that, I believe since 1930 or possibly earlier.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: 30 or more years.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: 30 or more years yes, sir many years.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: I asked because -- it rather interested me that they hadn't been construed until the last year or two. How many -- have been routinely a good many convictions under these.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: There have been some, not a greater amount.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Because it's wasn't until after certiorari was granted in this case as I understand it that one of these ordinances that is 1142 was construed to mean that the illegality of standing or loitering on a street was only relevant in connection with having previously blocked the sidewalk, block the respective sideway, that's the Meadowbrook case, isn't it?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And that came after certiorari was filed in this case.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, of course they say that.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And was not until the Phifer case that I understand it, 1231 was construed to be confined to vehicular traffic.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: So far as I know, that's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So this would suggest, I would guess that there haven't been many or it maybe any convictions under these statutes until recently.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, I am quite sure. I have been associated with the city for some years both presently and formerly and we have had some convictions on that, but I would say it has not been a common sort of a violation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: [Inaudible] one of Justice Stewart question --

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You said that they had ordinances from the city of Birmingham against loitering since the 1930s, didn't they have them back at 1910.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: What I was trying to say Mr. Justice Black was that I have checked it back that far, it goes back at least that far and maybe further.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: As I recall it, when I was a police [Inaudible] one of the most common charges was loitering on the sidewalk.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir, we have had it for many years.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, that is kind of a legacy statute, that's a little bit different, the common loitering statute.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, no I think the ordinance has presently worded, it is pretty well --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: The way it has been for example, but I would suggest there maybe other ordinances having to do with legacy of the town.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. McBee how wide is this sidewalk at that particular place.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: I believe the evidence does not show the exact width of the sidewalk. However, I would just estimate probably 10 to 12-feet wide, just an estimate.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: How far is this corner from the Court House that Shuttlesworth came from?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: He had been to the federal building which is on 19th and 5th avenue and he was on 19th and 2nd avenue which was some three or four blocks.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Three or Four blocks?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And do I understand this is was the same day that the United States District Court dismissed the other complaints against him in accordance with the mandate of this Court.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: I would not be able to say for sure. I think that is probably correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I thought I understood Mr. Nabrit.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: He may have said that.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: He may have said that and I am not sure now, there was an occasion, there was an occasion of that kind that did occur.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I see Mr. Nabrit shook his head. I'm probably wrong about it too.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: There was, I think maybe to clarify, there was more than one hearing in the matter before the District Court. And I think possibly on this occasion nothing happened on district court except that they reported and the matter was continued or something like that. But certainly, no evidence that it was the day could have been.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Mr. McBee?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes Mr. Justice Brennan.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Have there been other prosecutions under these ordinances?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: I beg your pardon sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Did I understand you to answer I think Justice Black there have been other prosecutions.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Oh yes sir, there have been, but no appeal so far as I know that went to our Appellate Courts.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Did anyone get six months for violation or any of these before?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, I don't know sir, I couldn't answer that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Have you ever heard one?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, of hand, I just couldn't say, I mean --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Maybe that's why there haven't been any appeals.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, that could be, I mean that it maybe that could be --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Would there be any record anywhere, I suppose this isn't a court of record there wouldn't be any record anywhere of the convictions of these ordinances?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: No sir, it's not a Court of record, no sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So unless there was an appeal, there wouldn't be anyway of our knowing any of this.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: There would be no way of really, I mean -- of really determining the facts of any given case, there would be no way that I know of.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Because it hasn't been and I'm right, am I not in my understanding that it hasn't been until the last year or two that these ordinances have been construed at all.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, if the petitioner says that's true and so far as I know it is, it could be, I certainly don't know anything on the contrary.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That is construed by any Court of record or anything for which we have a record.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: That's correct, other than -- it may have been, now I must explain this too under our procedure, the conviction from the Recorder's Court is appealed to the Circuit Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Then there is a trial de novo there.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: There is trial de novo there.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Yes, and that is a Court of record.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: That is a Court of record, but there is no, so far as I know, I mean we do not have any record of any -- they do not keep or they don't write opinions, the decisions are not reported in any of these reports.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: So it would be difficult to find, I mean in the actual cases on there. I don't know how much time I have left, but I did want to go to a comment on the point that is raised in reference to 1142. As we understand it, the Court of Appeals in this case said that 1142 applies not in freedom of the speech cases, but has its application only in, in the matter related to the blocking of the sidewalk or traffic situation in relation to sidewalks and streets. And on page 17 of our brief, we do quote the holding of the Court of Appeals or the reference by the Court of Appeals to Section 1142 as amended that [Inaudible] city code which states that it is not aimed at free speech and simply it is a municipal regulation, which is confined to the controller traffic and use of streets and sidewalks. The effort to bring into the picture a contention that Reverend Shuttlesworth, because of the fact that he had some notoriety as a civil rights leader in the city of Birmingham is, we think beside the point, because actually the undisputed testimony is that officer Byars who made the arrest and we're looking now at page 23 of the record near the bottom of the page, in which the question was asked, you were familiar with his face and features were you not of that is of Reverend Shuttlesworth and the answer was, I was not. Reverend Shuttlesworth testified in the case and he said that he didn't recognize officer Byars, he did not know officer Byars. Officer Byars said he did not know Shuttlesworth and the testimony of Shuttlesworth was that at no time, was he addressed that is, he Shuttlesworth addressed by his name. So the clear import of the evidence was, and so far as I know the uncontradicted evidence was that Mr Byars who made the arrest did not know Reverend Shuttlesworth.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Didn't Mr. Nabrit say that he also testified that he didn't know whether or not they were colored people.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, he said the entire group. There were number in the group, I mean of some 10 or 12 according his testimony. Now he did not say that he didn't know that Shuttlesworth and Phifer were colored people, but he did not know.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: They are colored, aren't they?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Actually I think that -- oh yes, that's correct, yes that's true.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I thought the question was whether or not there were any white people in the group.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: That's what he asked him and he said he didn't know. He couldn't tell about that, but I believe that another officer, officer Holman I believe said that they were all colored as far as he could tell. But in reference to Phifer, the same testimony, Mr Byars testified he did not know Reverend Phifer. Reverend Phifer testified he didn't know Mr. Byars and he did not call Mr Byars by, I mean Reverend Phifer by his name. So the evidence would clearly indicate that the fact that Reverend Shuttlesworth or Reverend Phifer, one of both were well known civil rights leaders had nothing actually to do with the arrest and with the trial of these defendants.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: [Inaudible] we didn't have to pass on that?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: I beg your pardon sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: [Inaudible] decide that one or the other do you?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: No sir, I don't think it's presented in the record, no sir. I agree on that. I certainly agree on that, it doesn't -- it is not presented in the record anywhere.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I did note one thing, I asked what was this all about, what they were trying to do and actually I'm unable to find anything in the record, and you said that it didn't show and the other side said it didn't show.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: It does not show.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The city kept it out, doesn't it?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: No sir, it was never -- as far as I know there were no efforts made to bring up what they were doing.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, it is your trying to bring out, was it not, that there was a selective buying campaign going on.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: But they brought that up Mr. Justice Black.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes, well the court sustained the objection to it.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: That is true.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But one of their assignments of error in the Supreme Court of Alabama.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: That is true, but that was merely a furtherance of what the Court felt was immaterial inquiry. The evidence had already been in that there was -- the rumor to be a selective buying campaign. I think one of the officers said that they had heard about it, but they were asked if they had received any instructions or official notice, they said they had not. They had been given no instructions to --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I'm not talking about what the officer said, but they did raise the question to try to prove that who that object [Inaudible] they were trying to protect their civil rights and then one of the assignments of errors that the Court of Appeals passed on, was saying the Court was correct in holding that, that evidence was not enough. I don't know the materiality of it and I wouldn't say so.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: I think what they were asking Mr. Justice Black, was had there been any of publication in the radio or newspaper about the release of these men from jail. I believe that's what the question involved was, there was a trial pending in the Court, is that correct? That's right on my release from jail. What was the circumstance of you being in there. In other words, and Mr. Walker objected to going in to a side issue of what he had been in jail about and about which they have done a hearing over in the Federal Court. That did not relate at all as I see it to any sort of civil rights movement or civil rights activity.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: [Inaudible] whole transcript or was this testimony that was given that doesn't included in the --

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: So far as I'm aware, it contains it all.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And there was no offer of proof available as to on this exclusion of evidence.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: No sir, the only -- there was not. This is all there is on the subject of that question, simply the question of whether or not this hearing over in the Federal Court had received wide publicity, that's all. And the court felt that was extraneous and of course the question -- the next question was, why was it necessary for you to leave 19th and go to 16th street and 5th Avenue to get a cup of coffee, that was another question which was not relevant to anything that went on there, why they left.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Back to your colloquy a few moments ago with Justice White.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes Sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: How is this matter left in your mind now? Do you think that we should take this case as involving convictions on the both counts or as you first indicated at least as I understood you that we can't tell whether the conviction rested on both counts.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, actually that I think is probably correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The latter?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: That you cannot tell with definite certainty. I would say that if there be a choice, you would have to say that since the conviction was on both counts and the conviction sustained on both counts that it was intended to be on both, but that's another –-

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, how do you think we should take as representing the state, because you said two opposite things.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: I am not quite aware.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I heard you said a moment ago that you thought, the proper thing to do is, on this record was to say or us to say that we could not tell whether the conviction was on the both counts or not.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, I guess, I would have to put it this way that I do not know that you can, but I think probably -- you will probably have to deal with the cases though the conviction were on both.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I don't understand that because when you say that you think we probably have to say on this record we cannot tell whether it is on both counts and I do don't see how we can go along and say well, we have to assume that it will.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well the record is not clear other than that mere inference, it has to be drawn from the fact that convicted as charged, that is the only thing I know of, which you would have to go with.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Alabama.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If the Judge finds fault on one side on all of the issues involved.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Frequently as charged guilty, as they did here.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If they have any one issue tried, for the party that's decided, for the party who wins, then that is settled.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And you can -- you do note that you can attribute it, that is the argument.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, it could be a do [Inaudible] that you can attribute it to a count that is good.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: It is good yes sir that is exactly right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I know that, that's true in civil cases.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, and it is true also, as we understand it in the criminal cases as well, because we have the line of cases of which we think do sustain that Classon versus United States and also Pierce versus United States and of course some Court of Appeals cases U.S. versus Parollo and Cliff versus United States, that I believe is -- I think the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals I'm not sure, but at any rate those cases, we say sustain the view point that if the Court even should determine that one of the cases is not sufficiently proven by the evidence that the conviction should be sustained by the good count unless we do get into a question of the constitutionality of the counts and the Court holds that the ordinances sustained was supporting either of the counts is unconstitutional, then I think you would have to reverse it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why would that differ -- require different in our cases of that principle, the fact that, that is one of the constitutional ground defect and the other is another defect.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, the only point that I can see is that historically the holding has been that either in the federal and criminal cases or several cases either one. If you have a good count to which the conviction can be sustained and you did not give him more than he was and that he could have received for that one count, then if you merely find that the evidence isn't insufficient to support, then you rest your decision on the good count and sustain them.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: There would be an element of uncertainty, I am not saying whether you are right or wrong. I asked you in the beginning, that question, because I wanted to ask you thought we would be governed by that rule, there is [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, I do think so.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: This degree of uncertainty.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: I do think that's true.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: They may have thought they were convicting him on both charges and may have given him.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Twice as much as they otherwise would have given him. What is that uncertainty?

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: The only thing is, he got no more than he could have gotten --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Yes, that is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, he might have got only a fine let's say, I suppose.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It might have been less.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, of course the -- I mean the record is clear. I mean, he was convicted on as charged in the complaint, I mean that is what we have to stand on.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Conviction was affirmed on both.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: I think it was, yes sir, I believe that, although I can't say for positive certainty, but I surely believe.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: We cannot know for whatever effect that it had, we cannot know that the Court would have imposed the penalty it did, if they had thought they could only be convicted on one.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: Well, that maybe the case. Yes sir.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Nabrit.

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. May I say at the outset, the petitioner objected pretrial to the constitutionality of Section 1231 in the very same terms, he objected to the constitutionality of Section 1142 and it's all on page six of the demurrer and also exactly the same way page four and the motion to quash. This last case of Shuttlesworth in this Court involved a similar sort of an attempt to prevent this Court from reaching the federal issue that would involved the wrong side of the paper. Well, this esoteric procedural rule, that a pleading that is labeled separate even though, it is nevertheless called joined and therefore improper is I submit, requires a meaningless ritual like this Court talked about in Staub against Baxley where the man had to write in separate sentences, I object to the constitutionality of this law and that law. Under -- but it is plain that the petitioner presented his constitutional objection to both statues and ample time for the Court to rule on them, to understand his objection very plainly. Now, I don't propose to devote much more time to a mystery of what he was convicted about. It seemed to me clear that the Court can't note that the prosecutor convicted him doesn't seem to know at this point, but I think both charges were constitutionally vulnerable. Section 1142, it is so obviously capable of sweeping and discriminatory application that if such a law as this were upheld, no civil rights worker could ever safely venture on the streets in a community where the police were disposed to suppress civil rights activity. The threat of serious penalties, here eight months in jail serves as deterrent to coerce to obey lawless police orders. As for example, police orders of a type in right against Georgia that is specifically designed to enforce segregation. And this one is obviously capable of application to First Amendment situation, to every kind of First Amendment situation on the streets. It makes the policemen the dictator of the streets without any possibility of a effective review by the courts. The very fact that under this Court's usual practice, the facts escapes strict review, demonstrates the necessity for a really strict standard of the statutory specificity with a law so easily susceptible of abuse and use to suppress free speech. I would point out however, that this Court's decisions in Edwards against South Carolina and Cox against Louisiana last term make a claim that the Court has the power and duty to scrutinize the record, that the fact should be taken as they appear in the record consistent with the findings by the Alabama Court of Appeals, but not limited to that Court's characterizations of that fact. Now, I believe that the Chief Justice asked Mr. McBee how wide the sidewalks are and he correctly responded that there is no evidence of the width of the sidewalks in the record and I understand he gave his own estimate. It is my understanding that his estimate is right, about 12 feet as to the second avenue side, by the 19th street sidewalk is very wide about, 20 feet wide.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: How wide?

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: It's around 20 feet wide, the length of that block.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: That's the one they were on?

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: That's the -- the 19 Street side, the Street they had been walking down and the -- it was Second Street --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Were they -- were they at the time of the crowd was assembled, were they on the 12th Street side or the 22 foot side?

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: They were at the intersection of these two, of these two street sidewalks.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: By the street on the --?

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: Yes, somewhere on that intersection, I do not know very clear. The -- I would conclude by suggesting that this prosecution of Fred Shuttlesworth and sentenced to eight months in jail and hard labor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I know if it is the original starts with a $100, because they paid a $100 fine.

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: I believe Your Honor, that the sentence in the Recorder's Court was also 180 days and a $100 fine, and --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is that in the Recorder – that's the maximum they can give this one, take out the --

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: As a matter of both counts.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: $100 and a 180 days, six months.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: On page 11, they said, you hereby fined a sum of $100 and plus and a new paragraph that said [Inaudible] fail to pay this fine of $100 and it costs $5 or confess judgment for goodness based on security is therefore considered by the court [Inaudible] law and then the defendant Shuttlesworth perform hard labor.

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: Mr. Justice Clark, the final, the sense in the recorded score as indicated on Page 2, the, this is the --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: This is the trial de novo here which sentence is in effect.

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: The second one, the one you were looking at. I thought the question was did he get a jail term in both courts, and the answer is yes, he got a 180 days in both courts, and the $100 fine in both courts.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And because he did not pay the $100 -- $100 fine, it was 52 days more, isn't that right?

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: I am unable to ascertain whether -- whether there are two $100 fines, I do not think so, I hope say.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: No, No. I didn't say that, I said he got --

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: If you do not pay the fine, you get the 2 months more.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, and having not already determined that because he has not paid the fine, then he has to serve 52 days at $0.75 a day.

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: I confess by the inaccuracy in understanding the Alabama judgment, it says that and then it says all confessed judgment with good sufficient cause, I just do not know what that mean.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: My question is when he -- if we were to affirm the case, if he paid a $100 would it be ending or would he have pay to serve the 52 days?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I could pay it.

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: I think he could pay.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: All it had to do was just pay $100 and the case would be, trial would be --?

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: He still has to serve 6 months.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: 52 days for fine if he could pay that.

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What you have as the same thing you have all over the country and that if a man is convicted by these fines, he does not pay the fine, he is sentenced to a certain amount of imprisonment. Here, they can give him 6 months in prison, 180 days and they can also fine him. If he doesn't pay the fine, then he has to serve the time, but he can pay the fine at any time.

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: Yes. We would in conclusion respectfully submit that this harsh stature is unconstitutionally vague. The statute that allows a policeman so much scope and authority, I could paraphrase the words that Mr. Justice Jackson in another context, the issue is well, the policeman is the law or whether he is under the law. This statute has no limitation to place the policeman under the law.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Mr. Nabrit did you ever press you objections, or press your point of the evidence that you offered was exclusion [Inaudible] constitutional term --

turns.name: JAMES M. NABRIT, III
turns.text: Not to my knowledge.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And you did not bring that point here again.

turns.name: EARL MCBEE
turns.text: It's not in the petition for certiorari.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Very well.
